title: The Baptism and Filling of the Holy Spirit
intro: There is a difference between having the Holy Spirit and being filled with the Holy Spirit. Every believer has the Spirit. But the filling — the ongoing, overflowing, empowered life in the Spirit — is something we are commanded to pursue. Not once. Continually.
reading:
Gavin's outline distinguishes between two distinct ministries: the baptism of the Spirit and the filling of the Spirit.

1 Corinthians 12:13 establishes the baptism as the work that places every believer into the body of Christ: in one Spirit we were all baptized into one body. This happens at salvation. It is universal to all who are in Christ.

But Ephesians 5:18 issues a command that goes beyond the initial work: be filled with the Spirit. The Greek verb is present continuous — keep on being filled. It is not a one-time experience to be settled into. It is a daily, ongoing posture of surrender and receptivity.

Acts 2:4 records the first dramatic filling at Pentecost. Acts 4:31 records it again in the same community, for the same people, in response to prayer and need. The disciples who were filled at Pentecost were filled again. The filling is not a ceiling — it is a fountain.

Luke 4:1 tells us that Jesus Himself was full of the Holy Spirit. The One who possessed the Spirit without measure still lived in daily dependence on the Spirit's leading. If the Son of God lived a Spirit-filled life, how much more do we need it.

Galatians 3:2 points to faith as the means. Luke 11:13 points to asking: how much more will the heavenly Father give the Holy Spirit to those who ask Him.
reflection: When did you last intentionally ask the Father to fill you afresh with the Holy Spirit? What would a posture of daily surrender and asking look like in your life?
abide: Father, fill me again. Not because my first filling was insufficient, but because I am a vessel that leaks — distracted, depleted, divided. I present myself to You again today. Fill me to overflowing.
